preFix.title: Wet-felted pot holder
preFix.subtitle: A dense flat disc of fulled merino, 20 cm diameter, for hot pots and pans
preFix.excerpt: A thick flat disc of fully fulled merino wool, 20 cm in diameter and at least 8 mm thick. Four cross-hatch layers of roving are wet-felted and fulled until the felt is dense enough to provide brief protection from heat up to 180°C.
preFix.sourceNotes: Flat wet-felted pot holders are an introductory project common in UK and European wet-felting classes. Technique synthesised from general wet-felting practice; not drawn from a specific PD source.

content.text: A pot holder made from fully
fulled

merino wool is thick, slightly compressible, and provides brief insulation between a hot pan and a wooden or stone surface. This one is a flat disc, 20 cm in diameter and approximately 8 mm thick when finished. No resist or shaping is involved: four
cross-hatch layers
of
roving
are laid flat, wetted, rubbed to
pre-felt
stage, and then fulled hard until the disc resists compression. The project takes about 90 minutes start to finish, making it a practical first felting project.
Method
Step 1: Lay the roving layers

Lay the felting mat on a table at a comfortable working height. Pull thin wisps of roving and lay them in a rough circle about 28 cm in diameter (the disc will shrink to approximately 20 cm during fulling). Lay the first layer with all wisps running left-to-right. Add a second layer with all wisps running top-to-bottom. Add a third layer left-to-right and a fourth top-to-bottom. Keep the layers even in thickness and distribute the roving so no area is noticeably thicker than another.
Step 2: Wet down and press

Add a few drops of olive-oil soap to warm water (around 40°C). Drizzle the soapy water slowly and evenly over the roving stack until every layer is saturated. Place a piece of netting or bubble wrap over the surface and press down with flat hands to push out air bubbles and make contact between fibres.
Step 3: Rub to pre-felt

Remove the netting and rub the surface with a flat hand using small circular movements, starting in the centre and working outward. Use light pressure for the first two minutes; the roving will stick to your palm initially. Once a surface skin begins to form (usually after 3-4 minutes), increase pressure and rub more vigorously. Rub for 10 minutes total. Flip the disc, check the underside, and rub the underside for a further 5 minutes. The disc has reached pre-felt when the fibres do not pull away when pinched lightly.
Step 4: Roll in the bamboo mat

Place the pre-felt disc on the bamboo rolling mat. Roll the mat tightly around the disc and roll it back and forth on the table with firm pressure for 5 minutes. Unroll, turn the disc 90 degrees, and roll again for 5 minutes. Repeat: turn 90 degrees, roll for 5 minutes, twice more. The disc will shrink noticeably during this stage.
Step 5: Full to density

Throw the disc firmly onto the felting mat 20 times, rotating it a quarter turn between throws. Then scrunch and squeeze it with both hands, working from the edges inward, for 5 minutes. Re-wet with hot soapy water if the disc begins to cool and dry out. Check the thickness with a measuring tape: the disc needs to be at least 8 mm at its thickest point. If it is thinner, continue rolling and throwing in 5-minute increments until the density is sufficient. A properly fulled pot holder will not compress more than 1-2 mm under firm hand pressure.
Step 6: Trim and rinse

If the edge is uneven, use scissors to trim to a neat circle (the felt is dense enough to cut cleanly once fully fulled). Rinse in water at the same temperature as the wash water (around 30°C). Squeeze out excess water without wringing. Lay flat on a towel and leave to dry at room temperature.

items.fix: Start with a very light circular motion for the first 3 minutes. Place a piece of netting over the surface and rub through the netting to distribute pressure more evenly.
items.cause: Rubbing pressure was too heavy before the fibres had time to begin bonding
items.problem: The roving pulls apart in clumps when rubbing begins

items.fix: For the next attempt, increase roving to 140 g. If the current disc is still workable, continue fulling: more throwing and rolling will compress and thicken the felt.
items.cause: Insufficient roving weight, or fulling was stopped too early
items.problem: The finished disc is thinner than 8 mm

items.fix: Turn the disc 90 degrees between every rolling session to distribute shrinkage evenly.
items.cause: Rolling was done in one direction only, causing more shrinkage along one axis
items.problem: The disc is oval rather than round

items.fix: In future, ensure the outer 2 cm of each layer overlaps slightly beyond the intended diameter. On the current disc, trim the loose edge with scissors once fully fulled.
items.cause: Roving was not laid far enough to the edges during the initial layers
items.problem: The edge is ragged and loose

content.text: Care and finishing

Hand-wash in cool water with a wool-safe detergent. Press flat and leave to dry. Do not tumble dry. The pot holder can be used on surfaces up to 180°C for brief contact, but it is not a replacement for an oven glove for gripping handles. If the surface becomes scuffed with use, a light buff with a damp cloth restores the finish.
